name: Islamic Tradition
uses: Sennosides: Natural compounds that stimulate intestinal movement and water secretion
Reliable Laxative: Recognized by modern medicine as effective for short-term constipation relief
Colon Cleansing: Used in medical settings to prepare for colonoscopy procedures
Weight Management: May support healthy elimination as part of balanced lifestyle
Antioxidants: Contains flavonoids and other beneficial plant compounds
Anti-parasitic: Traditional use for intestinal parasites supported by some studies
Short-term Only: Not intended for daily long-term use (maximum 1-2 weeks)
Start Low: Begin with minimal dose and adjust as needed
Adequate Hydration: Drink plenty of water when using senna
Timing: Take in evening for morning effect; avoid when traveling
Not for Children: Generally not recommended for children under 12 without medical guidance
Pregnancy: Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless prescribed
Medical Conditions: Consult healthcare provider if you have intestinal disorders, heart conditions, or take medications
rituals: Internal Purification: Cleansing the body mirrors spiritual purification of the soul
Prophetic Wisdom: Following natural medicine as taught by the Prophet
Balance and Moderation: Teaches the Islamic principle of avoiding extremes in treatment
Connection to Makkah: The name "Sana Makki" links this plant to the blessed city
Renewal: Periodic cleansing represents spiritual and physical renewal
Reliance on Natural Means: Using what Allah has provided in creation for healing
preparation: Senna Tea (Infusion): Steep 2-3 dried leaves or 1/2 teaspoon crushed leaves in hot water for 10-15 minutes. Drink before bedtime for morning relief
With Sanoot: Combine senna with black cumin or fennel seeds as recommended in the Hadith for enhanced effectiveness
Senna Pods: Use the dried fruit pods (gentler than leaves) by steeping 3-4 pods in water
With Prunes or Dates: Mix senna tea with dates or prunes to enhance taste and add gentle fiber
Cold Infusion: Soak leaves overnight in cool water for a milder preparation
With Ginger: Add fresh ginger to reduce potential cramping and improve digestion
Dosage: Start with smallest effective dose; do not exceed 7-10 consecutive days of use

description: Islamic Tradition is a sacred plant used in islamic spiritual practices.

keyMyths.title: The Prophet's Use of Miswak
keyMyths.description: The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) frequently used the miswak (a teeth-cleaning twig from a tree, often considered an herb) for oral hygiene and as a practice of purification, highlighting its importance in Islamic tradition. It's considered a sunnah (practice) of the Prophet.

keyMyths.title: Black Seed (Nigella Sativa) as a Cure
keyMyths.description: The black seed (Nigella sativa) is highly regarded in Islamic tradition, with prophetic narrations stating it has healing properties for all diseases except death. This belief has led to its widespread use in traditional medicine.

keyMyths.title: Henna and Beauty
keyMyths.description: Henna is used for beautification and celebrations, particularly during weddings and Eid. Its association with beauty and festivity is rooted in cultural practices adopted and incorporated within Islamic traditions.
keyMyths.source: Various cultural practices across Muslim-majority regions

keyMyths.title: Frankincense and Spiritual Cleansing
keyMyths.description: Frankincense, while not explicitly mentioned extensively in primary Islamic texts regarding specific rituals, is often used in some Sufi traditions and cultural practices for its aromatic qualities, believed to aid in spiritual cleansing and creating a conducive atmosphere for prayer and meditation.
keyMyths.source: Practices within certain Sufi orders and cultural traditions

keyMyths.title: Use of Herbs in Prophetic Medicine
keyMyths.description: Prophetic Medicine, or 'al-Tibb al-Nabawi', includes the use of various herbs and natural remedies recommended or used by the Prophet Muhammad for treating ailments. This tradition emphasizes natural healing and reliance on God's creation for well-being.

sources.name: Sahih al-Bukhari
sources.description: A collection of hadith (sayings and actions of the Prophet Muhammad) considered one of the most authentic sources in Sunni Islam.
sources.title: Sahih al-Bukhari

sources.name: Sahih Muslim
sources.description: Another highly regarded collection of hadith in Sunni Islam, offering insights into the Prophet's practices and teachings.
sources.title: Sahih Muslim

sources.name: Ibn Sina's 'The Canon of Medicine'
sources.description: A comprehensive medical encyclopedia written by the Persian physician Ibn Sina (Avicenna), influential in both Islamic and Western medicine, detailing the use of herbs and natural remedies.
sources.title: Ibn Sina's 'The Canon of Medicine'

sources.name: 'Prophetic Medicine' (al-Tibb al-Nabawi) literature
sources.description: A genre of Islamic literature focusing on the medical practices and remedies attributed to the Prophet Muhammad.
sources.title: 'Prophetic Medicine' (al-Tibb al-Nabawi) literature

sources.name: Cultural Practices Across Muslim-Majority Regions
sources.description: Observations and documentation of traditional customs and rituals involving herbs in various Muslim communities worldwide.
sources.title: Cultural Practices Across Muslim-Majority Regions
